papers.title: Deepfake Audio Detection Using Spectrogram-based Feature and Ensemble of Deep Learning Models
papers.abstract: In this paper, we propose a deep learning based system for the task of deepfake audio detection. In particular, the draw input audio is first transformed into various spectrograms using three transformation methods of Short-time Fourier Transform (STFT), Constant-Q Transform (CQT), Wavelet Transform (WT) combined with different auditory-based filters of Mel, Gammatone, linear filters (LF), and discrete cosine transform (DCT). Given the spectrograms, we evaluate a wide range of classification models based on three deep learning approaches. The first approach is to train directly the spectrograms using our proposed baseline models of CNN-based model (CNN-baseline), RNN-based model (RNN-baseline), C-RNN model (C-RNN baseline). Meanwhile, the second approach is transfer learning from computer vision models such as ResNet-18, MobileNet-V3, EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet-121, SuffleNet-V2, Swint, Convnext-Tiny, GoogLeNet, MNASsnet, RegNet. In the third approach, we leverage the state-of-the-art audio pre-trained models of Whisper, Seamless, Speechbrain, and Pyannote to extract audio embeddings from the input spectrograms. Then, the audio embeddings are explored by a Multilayer perceptron (MLP) model to detect the fake or real audio samples. Finally, high-performance deep learning models from these approaches are fused to achieve the best performance. We evaluated our proposed models on ASVspoof 2019 benchmark dataset. Our best ensemble model achieved an Equal Error Rate (EER) of 0.03, which is highly competitive to top-performing systems in the ASVspoofing 2019 challenge. Experimental results also highlight the potential of selective spectrograms and deep learning approaches to enhance the task of audio deepfake detection.

papers.title: Real-time Detection of AI-Generated Speech for DeepFake Voice Conversion
papers.abstract: There are growing implications surrounding generative AI in the speech domain that enable voice cloning and real-time voice conversion from one individual to another. This technology poses a significant ethical threat and could lead to breaches of privacy and misrepresentation, thus there is an urgent need for real-time detection of AI-generated speech for DeepFake Voice Conversion. To address the above emerging issues, the DEEP-VOICE dataset is generated in this study, comprised of real human speech from eight well-known figures and their speech converted to one another using Retrieval-based Voice Conversion. Presenting as a binary classification problem of whether the speech is real or AI-generated, statistical analysis of temporal audio features through t-testing reveals that there are significantly different distributions. Hyperparameter optimisation is implemented for machine learning models to identify the source of speech. Following the training of 208 individual machine learning models over 10-fold cross validation, it is found that the Extreme Gradient Boosting model can achieve an average classification accuracy of 99.3% and can classify speech in real-time, at around 0.004 milliseconds given one second of speech. All data generated for this study is released publicly for future research on AI speech detection.

This is essentially the same problem as the user's idea: 'A system that detects deepfake audio in real-time voice calls... distinguishing synthesized from genuine speech.' | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose a system for real-time deepfake audio detection using a binary classifier. The user specifies 'spectrogram features extracted from short audio segments' while the paper extracts 26 temporal audio features, including Chromagram, Spectral Centroid, and MFCCs, from '1-second of audio signal'. While both use feature extraction and binary classification, the specific features and the underlying models (e.g., XGBoost in the paper vs. an unspecified binary classifier for the user) represent meaningful differences in design within the same general method family. | Domain: The user's idea is a 'system that detects deepfake audio in real-time voice calls' and the paper's title is 'Real-time Detection of AI-Generated Speech for DeepFake Voice Conversion'. The paper explicitly states its goal is "real-time detection of AI-generated speech for DeepFake Voice Conversion" and demonstrates a system that "can classify speech in real-time, at around 0.004 milliseconds given one second of speech" using machine learning models trained on extracted audio features, which is precisely what the user proposes.

papers.title: Towards the Development of a Real-Time Deepfake Audio Detection System in Communication Platforms
papers.abstract: Deepfake audio poses a rising threat in communication platforms, necessitating real-time detection for audio stream integrity. Unlike traditional non-real-time approaches, this study assesses the viability of employing static deepfake audio detection models in real-time communication platforms. An executable software is developed for cross-platform compatibility, enabling real-time execution. Two deepfake audio detection models based on Resnet and LCNN architectures are implemented using the ASVspoof 2019 dataset, achieving benchmark performances compared to ASVspoof 2019 challenge baselines. The study proposes strategies and frameworks for enhancing these models, paving the way for real-time deepfake audio detection in communication platforms. This work contributes to the advancement of audio stream security, ensuring robust detection capabilities in dynamic, real-time communication scenarios.

papers.title: Audio Deepfake Detection Based on a Combination of F0 Information and Real Plus Imaginary Spectrogram Features
papers.abstract: Recently, pioneer research works have proposed a large number of acoustic features (log power spectrogram, linear frequency cepstral coefficients, constant Q cepstral coefficients, etc.) for audio deepfake detection, obtaining good performance, and showing that different subbands have different contributions to audio deepfake detection. However, this lacks an explanation of the specific information in the subband, and these features also lose information such as phase. Inspired by the mechanism of synthetic speech, the fundamental frequency (F0) information is used to improve the quality of synthetic speech, while the F0 of synthetic speech is still too average, which differs significantly from that of real speech. It is expected that F0 can be used as important information to discriminate between bonafide and fake speech, while this information cannot be used directly due to the irregular distribution of F0. Insteadly, the frequency band containing most of F0 is selected as the input feature. Meanwhile, to make full use of the phase and full-band information, we also propose to use real and imaginary spectrogram features as complementary input features and model the disjoint subbands separately. Finally, the results of F0, real and imaginary spectrogram features are fused. Experimental results on the ASVspoof 2019 LA dataset show that our proposed system is very effective for the audio deepfake detection task, achieving an equivalent error rate (EER) of 0.43%, which surpasses almost all systems.

The user's idea specifies a 'binary classifier on spectrogram features extracted from short audio segments,' while the paper uses 'F0 information and real plus imaginary spectrogram features' and 'squeeze-and-excitation ResNet (SENet) [8] is applied as the classifier of different subbands,' with a two-stage fusion. While both use spectrograms and classification, the paper's specific feature extraction (F0, real/imaginary spectrograms, subband modeling) and fusion approach introduce meaningful differences beyond a simple binary classifier on general spectrogram features. | Domain: The user's idea is a system that detects deepfake audio, and the paper's title is 'Audio Deepfake Detection Based on a Combination of F0 Information and Real Plus Imaginary Spectrogram Features'. The paper also mentions 'real-time voice calls' implicitly through its focus on 'spoofing attacks' against 'automatic speaker verification (ASV)' systems, which are often used in real-time scenarios. | Contribution: The user's idea proposes a system that detects deepfake audio by training a binary classifier on spectrogram features. The paper's core contribution is an 'audio deepfake detection method based on the F0 subband' which uses 'real and imaginary spectrogram features as complementary input features' and fuses them, achieving 'very effective' results. This directly matches the user's proposed system and methodology, including the use of spectrogram features and a classifier for deepfake detection.

papers.title: PITCH: AI-assisted Tagging of Deepfake Audio Calls using Challenge-Response
papers.abstract:
The rise of AI voice-cloning technology, particularly audio Real-time Deepfakes (RTDFs), has intensified social engineering attacks by enabling real-time voice impersonation that bypasses conventional enrollment-based authentication. This technology represents an existential threat to phone-based authentication systems, while total identity fraud losses reached $43 billion. Unlike traditional robocalls, these personalized AI-generated voice attacks target high-value accounts and circumvent existing defensive measures, creating an urgent cybersecurity challenge. To address this, we propose PITCH, a robust challenge-response method to detect and tag interactive deepfake audio calls. We developed a comprehensive taxonomy of audio challenges based on the human auditory system, linguistics, and environmental factors, yielding 20 prospective challenges. Testing against leading voice-cloning systems using a novel dataset (18,600 original and 1.6 million deepfake samples from 100 users), PITCH's challenges enhanced machine detection capabilities to 88.7% AUROC score, enabling us to identify 10 highly-effective challenges.
  For human evaluation, we filtered a challenging, balanced subset on which human evaluators independently achieved 72.6% accuracy, while machines scored 87.7%. Recognizing that call environments require human control, we developed a novel human-AI collaborative system that tags suspicious calls as "Deepfake-likely." Contrary to prior findings, we discovered that integrating human intuition with machine precision offers complementary advantages, giving users maximum control while boosting detection accuracy to 84.5%. This significant improvement situates PITCH's potential as an AI-assisted pre-screener for verifying calls, offering an adaptable approach to combat real-time voice-cloning attacks while maintaining human decision authority.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on a system that detects deepfake audio in real-time voice calls using a binary classifier on spectrogram features. The paper 'PITCH' also addresses the detection of real-time deepfake audio calls, but its core approach is a challenge-response method to amplify artifacts for detection, rather than solely relying on a binary classifier on spectrogram features. While both aim to detect real-time deepfakes, the paper's specific problem framing involves enhancing detection through challenges and human-AI collaboration, which is a different angle than the user's proposed method. | Method: The user's idea proposes a system that detects deepfake audio using a binary classifier trained on spectrogram features. The paper, PITCH, uses a challenge-response method to detect deepfakes, where challenges are designed to amplify artifacts for detection. While the paper does utilize Wav2Vec2 models as binary classifiers for 'Challenge Compliance' and mentions traditional deepfake detectors using MFCC/LFCC features, its core methodology is a challenge-response framework, which is distinct from the user's direct classification approach based solely on spectrogram features from short audio segments. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on detecting deepfake audio in real-time voice calls. The paper explicitly states its goal is to "detect interactive deepfake audio calls in real-world authentication scenarios" and addresses the "rise of AI voice-cloning technology, particularly audio Real-time Deepfakes (RTDFs)" in phone-based authentication systems, directly matching the user's specified application domain. | Contribution: The user's idea focuses on a real-time deepfake audio detection system using a binary classifier on spectrogram features. The paper 'PITCH' proposes a challenge-response method to detect deepfake audio calls, which is a different approach, although it does use machine detection capabilities (Wav2Vec2 model) as part of its framework. The core contribution of PITCH is the challenge-response mechanism and human-AI collaboration, not solely a real-time classifier on spectrogram features.

existing_examples.name: Deepfake Audio Detection Research
existing_examples.description: Numerous academic and industry research papers and projects focus on deepfake audio detection using machine learning, including binary classifiers on spectrograms. Examples include work from Google, Microsoft, and various university research groups (e.g., ASVspoof challenges).

existing_examples.name: Voice Biometrics/Authentication Systems
existing_examples.description: While primarily for identity verification, many advanced voice biometric systems incorporate liveness detection or anti-spoofing measures that can detect synthesized speech, often using similar feature extraction and classification techniques.

existing_examples.name: Commercial Anti-Spoofing Solutions
existing_examples.description: Companies like Pindrop, ID R&D, and Nuance offer anti-spoofing and fraud detection solutions for voice channels that aim to detect synthetic voices, although real-time deepfake detection is an evolving area.

reality_check.assessment: The core idea of detecting deepfake audio in real-time using a binary classifier on spectrogram features is a well-established and actively researched area. There are numerous academic papers, open-source projects, and commercial solutions that employ similar methodologies. The ASVspoof challenge series, for instance, has been a major driver in this field since 2015, specifically focusing on logical access (LA) and physical access (PA) spoofing, which includes synthesized speech (deepfakes).
novelty_aspects: Specific novel feature engineering or deep learning architectures for the classifier.
Unique dataset for training that addresses specific types of deepfakes or languages.
Integration into a specific real-time communication platform with minimal latency.
Achieving significantly higher accuracy or robustness against adversarial attacks than current state-of-the-art.
reality_check.recommendation: While the general concept exists, there is still room for innovation. Focus on a specific niche or a significant improvement. For example, can you achieve superior real-time performance, detect a new class of deepfakes, or integrate it seamlessly into a challenging environment? Review recent ASVspoof challenge winners and state-of-the-art papers to identify gaps or areas for improvement.

matched_sections.paper_title: Deepfake Audio Detection Using Spectrogram-based Feature and Ensemble of Deep Learning Models
matched_sections.heading: PROPOSED DEEP LEARNING BASED SYSTEMS
matched_sections.text_snippet: The high-level architecture of proposed deep learning based system for audio deepfake detection, which is denoted in Fig. 1, comprises two main parts: front-end spectrogram-based feature extraction and back-end deep learning model for classification. In particular, the draw input audio recordings are first split into 2-second segments. This segment length generally provides sufficient context to capture important features and allows faster training and inference for applications requiring real-time detection. Next, the 2-second audio segments are transformed into spectrograms. Finally, the spectrograms are explored by back-end deep learning models to detect real or fake audio segments.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly details a system that extracts spectrogram features from audio segments and uses deep learning models for classification, which is very similar to the user's proposed method.

matched_sections.paper_title: PITCH: AI-assisted Tagging of Deepfake Audio Calls using Challenge-Response
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Recent advancements in AI-generated voice cloning have blurred the line between authentic and fabricated speech [5, 18, 20, 42, 44]. This technological leap has particularly significant implications with the emergence of tools capable of generating convincing speech in real-time, fueling AI-generated impersonation scams. Such capabilities expose a critical vulnerability in billions of calls across contact centers, raising an urgent question: How can one ascertain the authenticity of a caller in an age where synthetic voices are indistinguishable from real ones?
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of detecting deepfake audio, specifically in real-time voice calls, due to the rise of AI voice cloning technology.

matched_sections.paper_title: PITCH: AI-assisted Tagging of Deepfake Audio Calls using Challenge-Response
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: The rise of AI voice-cloning technology, particularly audio Real-time Deepfakes (RTDFs), has intensified social engineering attacks by enabling real-time voice impersonation that bypasses conventional enrollment-based authentication. This technology represents an existential threat to phone-based authentication systems, while total identity fraud losses reached $43 billion.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states the problem of detecting real-time deepfake audio in phone-based authentication systems, which aligns with the user's goal of detecting deepfake audio in real-time voice calls.

While the paper's primary method is challenge-response, it mentions 'Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCC)' as a feature used in traditional deepfake detection, which are derived from spectrograms.
